<!-- Topic 2: std::array -->
<!-- Total slides: 5 -->

# std::array

---

## A fixed-size array that knows its own size? {.smaller}

- `std::array<T, N>` wraps a C array with STL features
- Same compile-time fixed size — but `.size()`, `.at()`, and no pointer decay

::: notes
Total slides: 5
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Declaration and initialization

```{.cpp}
#include <array>
using namespace std;

array<int, 5> scores = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};  // all 5 values
array<int, 5> zeros  = {};                     // all zeros
```

- Template parameters: element type first, then size
- Partial initializer list zero-fills the remaining elements, same as C arrays
- `array<int, 5>` and `array<int, 10>` are different types — size is part of the type

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Member functions

```{.cpp}
array<int, 5> scores = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};

scores.size();      // 5 — no pointer decay
scores.at(2);       // 84 — bounds-checked, throws if out of range
scores[2];          // 84 — no bounds check
scores.front();     // 91 — first element
scores.back();      // 62 — last element
```

- Works with range-for: `for (int s : scores)`
- Compatible with all STL algorithms (`sort`, `find`, etc.)

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Passing to a function

```{.cpp}
#include <array>
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void print(const array<int, 5>& arr);

int main() {
    array<int, 5> scores = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};
    print(scores);
    return 0;
}

void print(const array<int, 5>& arr) {
    for (int s : arr) {
        cout << s << "\n";
    }
}
```

- No size parameter needed — size is part of the type
- `const &` avoids copying the array

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Summary

- `std::array<T, N>` gives a fixed-size array `.size()`, `.at()`, and range-for support
- Unlike `int[]`, it does not decay to a pointer when passed to a function

<!-- Slide 5 -->
